name: Matrix Inverse & Solving Systems
whatIsIt: A square matrix A has an INVERSE A⁻¹ only when its DETERMINANT is non-zero (A is then 'non-singular'). The inverse is A⁻¹ = adj(A)/|A|, where adj(A) is the ADJOINT (the transpose of the cofactor matrix). Inverses let us solve a system of linear equations written as AX = B in one shot: X = A⁻¹B. CRAMER'S RULE gives each unknown as a ratio of determinants.
whyNeeded: Systems of equations model circuits, mixtures, economics and 3D geometry. The inverse and Cramer's rule solve them cleanly and are core Class 12 NCERT and JEE topics.
realLifeUses: Solving two or three equations from a word problem at once
Finding currents in an electrical network
Balancing a chemical or mixture problem
Computer graphics transforms that must be undone (inverted)

vocabulary.meaning: A number from a square matrix; for 2×2 [[a,b],[c,d]] it is ad − bc.
A matrix with |A| = 0 — it has NO inverse.
The transpose of the cofactor matrix; used to build the inverse.
adj(A)/|A|; satisfies A·A⁻¹ = I. Exists only when |A| ≠ 0.

story.title: Two Clues at Once
story.text: Sara has two equations in two unknowns. 'Substitute one into the other?' she asks. 'Or write them as AX = B and use X = A⁻¹B — solve both at once,' says Sir. He warns her: 'But first check |A|. If the determinant is zero, the matrix is singular and there's NO inverse.' Sara computes |A| = 10, not zero. 'Great — an inverse exists,' she smiles, 'so the system has one clean solution.'
story.extractedProblem: For A = [[3,1],[2,4]], what is the determinant |A|?

grids.label: |A| = ad − bc; inverse exists only if |A| ≠ 0

visual.caption: A 2×2 matrix [[a,b],[c,d]] has determinant ad − bc. If that is non-zero the matrix is invertible, and A⁻¹ = adj(A)/|A|.

standardMethod.summary: Compute |A|. If it is 0 the matrix is singular (no inverse). Otherwise A⁻¹ = adj(A)/|A|, and the system AX = B is solved by X = A⁻¹B (or by Cramer's rule with determinant ratios).
steps: If |A| ≠ 0, form the adjoint and compute A⁻¹ = adj(A)/|A|.
Solve AX = B by X = A⁻¹B, or use Cramer's rule x = |A₁|/|A|, y = |A₂|/|A|.

workedExamples.problem: A matrix has |A| = 0. Does it have an inverse?

commonMistakes.mistake: Thinking a matrix with |A| = 0 still has an inverse.
commonMistakes.fix: |A| = 0 means SINGULAR — there is NO inverse. An inverse exists only when |A| ≠ 0.
A⁻¹ = adj(A) DIVIDED by |A|, not multiplied.

easy.q: A matrix has an inverse only if |A| is…

questions.q: An inverse exists if and only if |A| is…
The formula for the inverse is…

teachBackPrompt: Write two of your own linear equations and set them up as AX = B. Explain to a grown-up how you'd first check |A| (must be non-zero), and then that X = A⁻¹B solves both equations at once.

revisionCard.summary: Square matrix A is invertible iff |A| ≠ 0 (non-singular). Then A⁻¹ = adj(A)/|A|. Solve AX = B by X = A⁻¹B, or Cramer's rule (determinant ratios). |A| = 0 ⇒ singular ⇒ no inverse. For 2×2, |A| = ad − bc.

meta.curriculum: CBSE Class 12 / JEE Main (inverse of a matrix, solving systems)

rememberIt.hook: A⁻¹ = adj(A)/|A|; exists iff |A| ≠ 0; solve AX=B by X=A⁻¹B
rememberIt.unpack: Zero determinant ⇒ singular ⇒ no inverse. Divide the adjoint by |A|, never multiply.

studentQuestions.a: Then the system has no unique solution (either none or infinitely many); the inverse method fails and you analyse it separately.
Both need |A| ≠ 0. The inverse (X = A⁻¹B) is handy if you'll reuse A⁻¹; Cramer's rule is quick for just finding the unknowns.

formulas.name: Inverse
formulas.whenToUse: Finding the inverse / solving AX = B.
